data.title: ICON leads a selloff among CROs after guidance cut; Baird downgrades
data.description:
ICON plc (NASDAQ:ICLR) traded sharply lower on Thursday, sparking a selloff among its rival contract research organizations after the Ireland-based company cut its guidance with its Q3 results. In reaction, Baird downgraded the stock to neutral from outperform.

Despite a ~20% YoY rise in net income, Icon’s (NASDAQ:ICLR) Q3 results missed Wall Street forecasts as its revenue contracted ~1% YoY to $2.0B.

“ICON’s results for the third quarter did not meet the expectations we had previously provided due to specific customer and division-level impacts,” CEO Steve Cutler remarked.

He attributed the revenue miss to various factors, including cautious R&D activity among biotechs and two large customers facing budget cuts and changes to their development models.

“We expect these impacts to continue into quarter four, and as a result, we are taking decisive action to realign our resources to forecasted activity,” Cutler added.

The company trimmed its full-year adjusted earnings per share guidance by $1.10 on both ends to $13.90-$14.10 and cut its revenue guidance by $190M-$250M to $8.260B-$8.300B, compared to $15.08 per share and $8.50B in the consensus, respectively.

“This is one of the worst relative performances we’ve seen from ICLR in eons,” Baird analyst Eric Coldwell wrote, downgrading the stock with a $340 per share target. “While we’ve been increasingly cautious on multiple fronts, at minimum we thought the company could protect profit; even that appears questionable,” Coldwell added.

With a gloomy industry outlook indicated in Icon’s (ICLR) results, its rivals have also joined the selloff. Notable decliners IQVIA Holdings (IQV) and Charles River Laboratories (CRL) have yet to report their Q3 results.

data.title: NextEra Partners raised at J.P. Morgan, seeing little downside left after big drop
data.description:
NextEra Energy Partners (NYSE:NEP) -2.9% in Thursday's trading, extending the 16% plunge from the previous session, after posting Q3 results that came in well below expectations.

Seeing little downside remaining after the stock's sharp drop and believing continued ownership by NextEra Energy (NEE) is now a more likely outcome, J.P. Morgan upgrades NextEra Partners (NEP) to Neutral from Underweight with a $22 price target.

JPM's Mark Strouse says near-term risk remains around NextEra Partners' (NEP) longer-term CEPF obligations and its ability to accretively finance potential dropdown acquisitions, while seeing a chance that "NEP can stabilize its cost of capital, grow the portfolio, and perhaps re-establish a virtuous cycle after a likely one-time reset to CAFD and DPU in order to satisfy CEPF obligations."

Strouse also believes a potential dropdown announcement, in conjunction with a DPU reset, could be a catalyst for the stock and provide increased growth visibility into FY 2026 and beyond.

data.title: Upstart expands to super-prime borrowers
data.description:
Upstart Holdings (NASDAQ:UPST) , which has focused its AI lending platform on underserved consumers, is expanding to more affluent borrowers with its new T-Prime lending platform, the company said Thursday.

"With our expansion into T-Prime, we're helping our bank and credit union partners leverage their competitive cost of funding to win the business of our nation’s more affluent borrowers," said co-founder and CEO Dave Girouard.

T-Prime focuses on the almost half of Americans who have credit scores above 720, a demographic that money center banks and fintechs common served.

"By partnering with Upstart in T-Prime, we’re able to offer best-in-class loan options, providing more flexibility, better rates, and broader access, nationwide," said Dennis Devine, president and CEO of Alliant Credit Union.

Upstart (NASDAQ:UPST) stock rose 2.3% in Thursday afternoon trading.

data.title: Baxter to restart hurricane-damaged NC plant next week
data.description:
Baxter (NYSE:BAX) intends to restart its North Cove manufacturing facility in North Carolina, which was knocked offline by Hurricane Helene, by the end of the next week, but is still unsure when production will be restored to pre-hurricane levels.

The company said that staffing at the plant, which was damaged by flooding during the storm, would return to pre-hurricane levels by the end of this week. It hopes to restart production of the highest-throughput IV solutions within next week, according to an update.

“It is important to note that the earliest that new North Cove product could begin entering the distribution channel is mid- to late-November. This is, however, ahead of our original expectations,” Baxter (BAX) said in the update.

The plant is the nation’s largest producer of intravenous and peritoneal dialysis solutions. The temporary closure of the plant has resulted in Baxter (BAX) employing a variety of inventory management and allocation measures to help minimize disruptions to patient care.

Baxter (BAX) noted that the FDA has temporarily authorized the importation of certain products made at its plants in Thailand and Singapore. The agency had already authorized importations from its plants in Ireland, China, Canada, the U.K., Spain and Mexico.

“Our goal remains to return to 90% to 100% allocation of certain IV solution product codes for U.S. customers by the end of 2024,” Baxter (BAX) added.

Baxter (BAX) announced the closure of the North Cove facility, which employs around 2,500 people, on Sept. 29.

The company has said it expects the closure to have a negative impact on its financial results. Baxter (BAX) is expected to release its Q3 earnings report on Nov. 8.

data.title: Nordic Semiconductor ASA reports Q3 results
data.description:
• Nordic Semiconductor ASA (OTCPK:NDCVF): Q3 Revenue of USD 159 million, up 18% from Q3 2023.
  • The reported profit before tax was USD 5.5M in Q3 2024.

data.title: Investor sentiment will remain vulnerable going into election, Fed cuts, geopolitics
data.description:
Investor risk sentiment is under pressure, according to a UBS note, due to rising Treasury yields and the S&P 500 (SP500) falling for a third consecutive day on Wednesday.

For the S&P 500 (SP500), this is the longest streak of losses since the start of September, although the index is currently up 0.23% for the day at 5,810.

The yield on the U.S. 10-year Treasury (US10Y) is up 14 basis points over the past three sessions, up 46 basis points this month. It is currently at 4.19%, down 1.18% on the day so far.

Also, the Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate (known as the MOVE Index for bond market volatility) rose at the highest level this year, while the VIX Index (VIX) remains above 2024 lows and close to its long-run average of around 20, Solita Marcelli, chief investment officer, Americas, at UBS, said in a note.

“The combination of higher yields, expectations for a more modest pace of Federal Reserve interest rate cuts, geopolitical concerns, and U.S. election uncertainty has kept markets on edge,” Marcelli said, adding that investors should continue to expect the market sentiment to stay vulnerable.

UBS analysts see the S&P 500 (SP500) reaching 6,600 by the end of 2025.

Treasuries yields: (US10Y), (US30Y), (US5Y).

Treasury ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), (BIL).

data.title: Wall Street mixed as Tesla boosts Nasdaq, while IBM, Honeywell weigh on Dow
data.description:
Wall Street's three major averages were mixed on Thursday. A post-earnings surge in Tesla (TSLA) propped up the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND), but that was countered by a slide in IBM (IBM) and Honeywell (HON) which weighed on the blue-chip Dow (DJI).

Meanwhile, a relentless bond sell-off that started this week partly due to a recalibration of Federal Reserve rate cut expectations finally stalled.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) seesawed, last +0.18% at 5,807.89 points in afternoon trade, while the Dow (DJI) was -0.35% at 42,364.81 points. The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was +0.61% at 18,387.92 points.

Of the 11 sectors, eight were in the red.

Tesla (TSLA) was among the top percentage gainers on both the S&P and the Nasdaq. The electric vehicle maker's stock soared +20.9%, after the company posted one of its strongest quarterly reports in a while and top boss Elon Musk projected as much as 30% deliveries growth in 2025.

However, the positive sentiment around Tesla (TSLA) failed to carry over to all of its Magnificent 7 peers, with the other members of the club putting in a mixed showing.

Some of the gains from Tesla (TSLA) were offset by a -6.8% slump in IBM (IBM). The legacy tech firm reported a quarterly top-line miss, as a solid increase in revenue in its software business was offset by flat sales in its consulting segment and a revenue dip in its infrastructure unit.

IBM (IBM) was the biggest drag on the blue-chip Dow (DJI), followed by Honeywell (HON). The industrial conglomerate shed -4.5% after cutting its full-year revenue guidance.

A -1.6% dip in Boeing (BA) also put pressure on the Dow (DJI). The troubled planemaker a day ago disclosed a whopping $6B in quarterly losses due to a crippling machinists' strike. Then, late Wednesday, in another blow to the company, the factory workers voted to reject a new contract offer from the firm that would have ended a six-week strike.

In other earnings-related moves, UPS (UPS) was +4.8%, after the parcel delivery giant and economic bellwether returned to revenue and profit growth after nearly two years.

Turning to the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields fell as a weekly bond sell-off lost steam. Some of that selling had been attributed to traders steadily recalibrating their expectations for aggressive Fed rate cuts following strong economic data recently. Comments from well-known Fed speakers this week have expressed a preference for gradual policy easing.

The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was down 5 basis points to 4.19%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was also down 5 basis points to 4.05%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

Thursday's economic calendar was fairly busy. Before the opening bell, data showed the number of Americans filing for initial jobless claims in the past week unexpectedly fell to 227K.

"It doesn't take much fancy analysis to see that today's initial jobless claims number was much better than expected (227K vs 242K consensus), particularly given the impact of hurricanes and strikes / furloughs. Here's my fancy analysis anyway to get a pulse check on just how much better than expected claims were: the aforementioned transitory factors still boosted initial jobless claims by ~12k during the week ending Oct 19, down from ~18k the week before," Parker Ross, global chief economist at Arch Capital Group, said on X (formerly Twitter).

"Thus, today's initial claims data was one of the best prints of the year when accounting for these recent one-offs," Ross added.

Shortly after the start of regular trading, S&P Global reported an improvement in Flash U.S. Composite PMI in October, signaling a "sustained solid expansion of business activity at the start of the fourth quarter."

That was followed by the U.S. Census Bureau's update on U.S. new home sales, which powered past estimates in September.

More on the markets

  • S&P Equal Weight Vs. Cap Weight
  • Turnaround Tuesday Comes Late
  • Bond market 'sniffing out Trump win' contributes to 10-year yield visiting 3-month high
  • SP500: Bracing For Turbulence

data.title: Virtu Financial shares drop despite Q3 earnings, revenue beat
Centene is latest to legally challenge Medicare Star Ratings
data.description:
Centene (NYSE:CNC) has become the latest health insurer to take the U.S. Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) to court over the agency’s Medicare Advantage Star Ratings for next year.

According to a lawsuit filed in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri, the managed care company accused CMS of unfairly downgrading its Star Ratings, leading to a loss of up to $73M in gross revenue.

The Medicare Advantage Star Ratings System is designed to give annual bonus payments to health plans with the highest ratings.

In its lawsuit, Centene (NYSE:CNC) accused CMS of using a single secret shopper call that failed to reach its call center in its Star Ratings calculations, noting that a software issue at the agency was to blame for the failure.

“CMS has decided to hold a single call against [the] plaintiffs, despite the fact that the call never reached [the] plaintiffs’ call center, and there was no call failure due to [the] the plaintiffs,” the company said.

“Rather, the CMS secret shopper’s own call notes show that the shopper’s ‘chat window closed unexpectedly,’ which is a clear failure of CMS’ software,” Centene (CNC) added.

Over the past few weeks, UnitedHealthcare (UNH) and Humana (HUM) have also filed lawsuits against CMS’s Star Ratings calculations for next year.
